expectations.text: Reads PRD and scope doc before asking questions — references specific FRs, user stories, or scope decisions
expectations.evidence: Transcript opening message: 'I've read the PRD, scope doc, and explored the codebase. Requirements call for waiver template CRUD, a public signing page at {org-slug}.rangeops.com/waiver, auto-customer creation, waiver status badges, and guardian/minor flows.' Throughout the interview, specific FRs are referenced: FR-9 (line 135/152), FR-23 (line 170), FR-4 (line 143/146), NFR-6 (line 123). Design doc maps FRs explicitly to behavior specs (e.g., 'PRD coverage: FR-1, FR-2, FR-3') and build phases. User stories referenced by name throughout (e.g., 'User Story: Create Waiver Template', 'User Story: Sign Waiver (Adult)').

expectations.text: Covers all 10 technical domains: architecture, data/state, core behavior, edge cases, integration, operational, security, testing, code design, phased build
expectations.evidence: Transcript has explicit section headings for all 10 domains: 'Domain 1: Architecture & Patterns' (Q1-Q3), 'Domain 2: Data & State' (Q4-Q7), 'Domain 3: Core Behavior (Technical)' (Q8-Q11), 'Domain 4: Edge Cases & Failure Modes' (Q12-Q15), 'Domain 5: Integration & Dependencies' (Q16-Q18), 'Domain 6: Operational & Rollout' (Q19-Q20), 'Domain 7: Security & Access' (Q21-Q23), 'Domain 8: Testing Strategy' (Q24), 'Domain 9: Code Design & Boundaries' (Q25-Q26), 'Domain 10: Phased Build Plan' (Q27-Q31). metrics.json confirms domainsCovered: 10 with all resolved: true.

expectations.text: Design references specific rangeops files, patterns, and conventions (schema patterns, service layer, auth, existing models)
expectations.evidence: Transcript references specific files and patterns: 'proxy.ts rewrites subdomain requests' (line 14), 'settings-tabs.tsx' (line 29), 'lib/customers.ts' pattern (line 34/37), 'lib/waivers.ts' (line 37), Drizzle ORM schema pattern, Effect + DbService + tagged errors (line 35), 'customer_organization_id_email_idx' index (line 168), 'permissions.ts' stubs for waiver_template and waiver (line 272-275), '@workspace/email' package (line 228), 'VerificationEmail, InvitationEmail' templates as precedent (line 229), 'customer-list.test.ts' pattern (line 291), 'createTestSession/addMember/resetTestState' helpers (line 300). Design doc references lib/db/schema/waivers.ts, lib/validation.ts, lib/errors.ts, lib/id.ts with specific prefix conventions.

expectations.text: Adversarial review runs automatically after design — delivers Ready/Revise/Rethink verdict with strengths and issues
expectations.evidence: review.md exists as a separate output file. It opens with 'Adversarial Review: Digital Waivers Design', delivers 'Verdict: Ready', lists 4 strengths (fully additive design, leverages existing patterns, frozen template snapshot, derived waiver status), and 4 severity-ordered issues (HTML sanitization, WCAG AA compliance, minor customer matching, resetTestState table list). It also identifies 2 risks (Tiptap bundle size, precedent-setting for public pages). The review references verification against codebase artifacts (permissions.ts, proxy.ts, organizationSetting.featureFlags, customer table, email package, Effect service pattern, test helpers).

expectations.text: Design maps back to PRD requirements — FRs and user stories referenced in behavior specs or build phases
expectations.evidence: Every behavior spec in design.md has a 'PRD coverage' line: Create Template maps to 'FR-1, FR-2, FR-3, User Story: Create Waiver Template'; Preview maps to 'FR-5'; Publish maps to 'FR-3, FR-4'; Edit maps to 'FR-4, User Story: Edit and Republish Template'; Sign Waiver (Adult) maps to 'FR-7 through FR-17, FR-23, FR-24, FR-28, User Story: Sign Waiver (Adult), Auto-Link and Create Customers'; Sign Waiver (Guardian) maps to 'FR-18 through FR-22'; Check Waiver Status maps to 'FR-25, FR-26, FR-29, FR-31'; View Coverage maps to 'FR-27'. Build phases also include PRD coverage fields: Phase 1 covers FR-1 through FR-6, Phase 2 covers FR-7 through FR-24/FR-28-30, Phase 3 covers FR-25-27/FR-31. metrics.json claims 31/31 FRs and 7/7 user stories covered.

expectations.text: Design doc has key sections: Decisions Log, Data Models, Behavior Specs, Edge Cases table, Phased Build Plan with acceptance criteria
expectations.evidence: design.md contains all required sections: 'Decisions Log' (16 numbered decisions with rationale), 'Data Models' (WaiverTemplate and Waiver schemas with fields, indexes, relationships, migration notes; Customer modifications), 'Behavior Specs' (7 specs: Create Template, Preview, Publish, Edit Published, Sign Waiver Adult, Sign Waiver Guardian/Minor, Check Status, View Coverage), 'Edge Cases & Failure Modes' (markdown table with 12 scenarios including Scenario, Expected Behavior, Severity columns), 'Phased Build Plan' with 3 phases each having Depends on, Decisions, PRD coverage, What, and checkbox-style Acceptance criteria. Additional sections include Scope, Assumptions & Unknowns, Security & Access, Testing Strategy, Code Design & Boundaries, and Pipeline Status.

expectations.text: Suggests /plan as next step when Ready
expectations.evidence: review.md final line: 'Recommended next step — Spec is ready. Run /plan to decompose into implementation issues.' Also in metrics.json: 'planSuggested: true, planSuggestionText: "Spec is ready. Run /plan to decompose into implementation issues."'

expectations.text: Glossary conditions checked — generates if needed, uses canonical terms in data models
expectations.evidence: glossary.md exists and documents the evaluation: 'The skill's glossary check triggers when: PRD introduces 3+ domain nouns not in codebase, OR naming conflicts between PRD/code/UI detected.' It lists the assessment: 'The PRD introduces domain nouns: waiver template, waiver, signing page, e-signature, guardian, minor' but concludes 'these terms are unambiguous in context and map directly to new schema objects.' It checks for naming conflicts against codebase terms (customer, organization, member, location) and permissions stubs. Decision: 'Glossary generation was not required.' The design doc uses canonical terms matching both PRD and schema (waiver_template, waiver, customer). This is a genuine evaluation, not just a rubber stamp — it explains the trigger conditions and why they weren't met.

claims.claim: All 31 functional requirements mapped to behavior specs and/or build phases
claims.evidence: design.md behavior specs have PRD coverage lines referencing FR-1 through FR-31. Build phases also reference FR ranges. The review.md confirms: 'All 31 functional requirements (FR-1 through FR-31) mapped to behavior specs and/or build phases.' Cross-checked: FR-1 through FR-6 in Create/Preview/Publish/Edit specs, FR-7-17/23-24/28 in Sign Waiver (Adult), FR-18-22 in Guardian/Minor, FR-25-27/29/31 in Check Status and Coverage. FR-30 covered in Phase 2 acceptance criteria.

claims.claim: All 7 user stories have explicit behavior specs
claims.evidence: design.md behavior specs reference: Create Waiver Template, Edit and Republish Template, Sign Waiver (Adult), Sign Waiver (Guardian for Minor), Auto-Link and Create Customers, Check Waiver Status at Check-In, View Waiver Coverage — all 7 named user stories.

claims.claim: permissions.ts stubs confirmed to match design
claims.evidence: The review claims 'permissions.ts stubs confirmed: waiver_template: ["create", "read", "update", "publish"] and waiver: ["create", "read", "list"]'. This is stated in the transcript (line 272) and review, but I cannot independently verify against the actual codebase since this is a simulated execution. Taking the transcript at face value as is standard for these evaluations.

claims.claim: proxy.ts rewrite already handles the subdomain routing needed
claims.evidence: Claimed in transcript line 14: 'proxy.ts rewrites subdomain requests into [orgSlug] segments' and review: 'proxy.ts rewrite confirmed'. Cannot independently verify against codebase. Consistent across transcript, design, and review.

claims.claim: Customer table already has all needed fields (firstName, lastName, email, phone, dateOfBirth, status)
claims.evidence: Stated in transcript line 45-46 and confirmed in review. Cannot verify against actual schema. Internally consistent across all outputs.

claims.claim: Email confirmation was moved to Phase 2 per user request
claims.evidence: Transcript line 362: User says 'let's put email confirmation in phase 2.' Assistant responds 'Updated. Email confirmation moves to phase 2.' Design doc Phase 2 acceptance criteria includes 'Email confirmation sent (failure doesn't block signing)' and Phase 2 description mentions 'Email confirmation via new Resend template.'

Transcript contains questions numbered Q1 through Q31. Manually confirmed: Q1-Q3 (architecture), Q4-Q7 (data), Q8-Q11 (core behavior), Q12-Q15 (edge cases), Q16-Q18 (integration), Q19-Q20 (operational), Q21-Q23 (security), Q24 (testing), Q25-Q26 (code design), Q27-Q31 (phased build). Count: 31.

suggestions.assertion: Covers all 10 technical domains: architecture, data/state, core behavior, edge cases, integration, operational, security, testing, code design, phased build
suggestions.reason: This assertion can pass just by having 10 section headers in the transcript. A stronger version would check that each domain has at least N substantive questions AND that the design doc has corresponding content for each domain. The current version could pass with token coverage (one trivial question per domain).

suggestions.assertion: Glossary conditions checked — generates if needed, uses canonical terms in data models
suggestions.reason: This eval ran against a case where the glossary was NOT needed, so we only verified the 'check and skip' path. To fully validate this assertion, a separate eval should use a PRD with ambiguous or conflicting terminology that triggers actual glossary generation. As-is, this only tests the negative case.

No assertion checks quality or depth of the adversarial review. The review found 4 issues, but no expectation verifies whether those issues are substantive or correct. An assertion like 'adversarial review identifies at least one security or data integrity issue not already in the design decisions' would test that the review adds genuine value rather than rubber-stamping.

No assertion checks that the AskUserQuestion tool was used for every question (a CLAUDE.md requirement). The transcript shows Q&A pairs but doesn't expose tool calls. If this is an interview-style skill, verifying AskUserQuestion usage matters — a skill could achieve the same transcript by asking questions in plain text, violating the convention.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-chosen and cover the key outcomes comprehensively. The main gap is that several assertions can be satisfied by surface structure (section headers, keyword presence) without verifying depth of content. The glossary assertion only tests the skip path, and the adversarial review assertion checks for existence but not quality. Adding a codebase-verification assertion (e.g., 'design references at least 5 specific file paths that exist in the codebase') would be high-value since codebase grounding is a core differentiator.
